key_insight: XRDiff uses a diffusion model to infer crystal structures directly from powder X-ray diffraction patterns, learning to resolve polymorphs from the diffraction signal.
engineering_relevance: This could enable faster, more automated crystal structure solution from PXRD in materials discovery and characterization, including cases with incomplete composition information.
method: Train on simulated structure–spectrum pairs to learn a PXRD-to-structure prior.
Condition the diffusion model on either full stoichiometry or only elemental constituents plus atom count.
Compare full-spectrum encodings with peak-based descriptors for robustness to experimental noise.
evidence_signal: The model is evaluated on composition-held-out polymorph benchmarks and on experimental PXRD generalization, where peak-based encoding performs best.
watch_item: Performance may drop when the experimental diffraction signal deviates from the simulated training distribution, so noise/artifact robustness remains a key deployment concern.
paper_title: XRDiff: Crystal Structure Prediction from Powder X-Ray Diffraction Data Using Diffusion Models
